paragraphs.context: Illustrations. (a) A threatens to publish a defamatory libel concerning Z unless Z gives him money. He thus induces Z to give him money. A has committed extortion. (b) A threatens Z that he will keep Z’s child in wrongful confinement, unless Z will sign and deliver to A a promissory note binding Z to pay certain monies to A. Z signs and delivers the note. A has committed extortion. (c) A threatens to send club-men to plough up Z’s field unless Z will sign and deliver to B a bond binding Z under a penalty to deliver certain produce to B, and thereby induces Z to sign and deliver the bond. A has committed extortion. (d) A, by putting Z in fear of grievous hurt, dishonestly induces Z to sign or affix his seal to a blank paper and deliver it to A. Z signs and delivers the paper to A. Here, as the paper so signed may be converted into a valuable security, A has committed extortion. (e) A threatens Z by sending a message through an electronic device that “Your child is in my possession, and will be put to death unless you send me one lakh rupees.” A thus induces Z to give him money. A has committed extortion.

Here A has extorted the purse from Z by putting him in fear of instant hurt, and being at the time of committing the extortion in his presence. A has therefore committed robbery. (c) A meets Z and Z’s child on the high road. A takes the child, and threatens to fling it down a precipice, unless Z delivers his purse. Z, in consequence, delivers his purse. Here A has extorted the purse from Z, by causing Z to be in fear of instant hurt to the child who is there present. A has therefore committed robbery on Z. (d) A obtains property from Z by saying—'Your child is in the hands of my gang, and will be put to death unless you send us ten thousand rupees.' paragraphs.context:
Examples:
(a) A takes Z’s property, thinking it’s his own, but later finds out it belongs to Z. If A then keeps it for himself, he’s guilty.
(b) A, thinking he has permission from Z to borrow a book, takes it without asking. If A later sells the book, he is guilty.
(c) A and B own a horse together. A takes the horse from B to use it. But if A sells the horse and keeps the money, he is guilty.

qas.question: What are some examples of misappropriation of property?

answers.text: Examples of misappropriation include taking someone else's property, thinking it's your own, and then keeping it, or taking a property without permission and selling it.

paragraphs.context:
Explanations: Even if someone takes property intending to return it, using it for themselves makes them guilty of misappropriation.
Example: A finds a government note belonging to Z and uses it as security for a loan, intending to return it later. A has committed an offense.
If someone finds property and takes it to protect it or return it to the owner, it’s not an offense. But if they take it for themselves or don’t try to find the owner, they’re guilty.
Example: A finds a rupee on the road, not knowing who it belongs to. A has not committed an offense.
Example: A finds a letter with a bank note and figures out who it belongs to but keeps the money. A is guilty.

qas.question: When is it considered misappropriation of property?

answers.text: It is considered misappropriation if someone takes property with the intent to use it for themselves or does not make efforts to return it, even if they initially intended to return it.

If the person was employed by the deceased (as a clerk or servant), the punishment can extend to seven years.

paragraphs.context: Example: Z dies leaving behind furniture and money. His servant A takes the money before it is legally handed over to the rightful person. A is guilty under this section.

paragraphs.context: Section 316: If someone is entrusted with property or authority over property and dishonestly uses or takes that property for themselves, or fails to follow legal or contractual instructions on how to handle it, they commit criminal breach of trust. If they allow someone else to do this, they are also guilty.

qas.question: What constitutes criminal breach of trust under Section 316?

answers.text: Criminal breach of trust occurs when someone entrusted with property or authority over property dishonestly uses or takes the property for themselves or fails to follow legal or contractual instructions on handling it.

paragraphs.context: Examples: If an employer deducts the employee's contribution for a Provident Fund but does not deposit it into the Fund, they have misused that money, which is a breach of trust. If an employer deducts the employee's contribution for the Employees' State Insurance Fund but doesn’t transfer it to the Fund, they are also guilty of criminal breach of trust.

qas.question: What are examples of criminal breach of trust under Section 316?

answers.text: Examples of criminal breach of trust under Section 316 include an employer misusing the Provident Fund or Employees' State Insurance Fund contributions by failing to deposit them as required.

paragraphs.context: Illustration: (a) A is an executor of a will and is supposed to distribute the deceased person's belongings according to the will. If A takes the property for themselves instead of following the will, A is guilty of criminal breach of trust.

paragraphs.context: In summary, if you are given responsibility over property or funds and misuse or fail to follow the rules for handling them, you are committing criminal breach of trust.

answers.text: The summary of Section 316 is that if you are entrusted with property or funds and misuse or fail to follow instructions on handling them, you commit criminal breach of trust.

qas.question: What defines stolen property under Section 317?

answers.text: Stolen property is property obtained through theft, extortion, robbery, cheating, or criminal breach of trust, whether the act occurred in India or elsewhere. If legally entitled, it is no longer considered stolen.

paragraphs.context: Punishment for Receiving Stolen Property: If someone dishonestly receives or retains stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe it is stolen, they can be punished with imprisonment for up to 3 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for receiving stolen property under Section 317?

answers.text: The punishment for receiving stolen property under Section 317 is imprisonment for up to 3 years, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Punishment for Receiving Stolen Property from Dacoits or Habitually: Receiving from a Dacoit: If someone dishonestly receives stolen property from a person whom they know or have reason to believe is part of a gang of dacoits, and they know or believe the property to be stolen, they can be punished with life imprisonment or rigorous imprisonment for up to 10 years, along with a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for receiving stolen property from dacoits?

answers.text: The punishment for receiving stolen property from a dacoit is life imprisonment or rigorous imprisonment for up to 10 years, along with a fine.

paragraphs.context: Habitual Dealing in Stolen Property: Anyone who habitually receives or deals in property they know or believe to be stolen can be punished with life imprisonment or imprisonment for up to 10 years, along with a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for habitual dealing in stolen property?

answers.text: The punishment for habitual dealing in stolen property is life imprisonment or imprisonment for up to 10 years, along with a fine.

paragraphs.context: Concealing or Disposing of Stolen Property: Anyone who voluntarily helps conceal, dispose of, or make away with stolen property, knowing or believing it to be stolen, can be punished with up to 3 years imprisonment, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for concealing or disposing of stolen property?

answers.text: The punishment for concealing or disposing of stolen property is up to 3 years imprisonment, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Definition of Cheating: A person is said to cheat when they deceive another person by fraudulently or dishonestly inducing them to: Deliver property to someone, Consent to someone retaining property, Do or omit something they wouldn’t normally do or omit, resulting in harm to their body, mind, reputation, or property.

qas.question: What is the definition of cheating under Section 318?

answers.text: Cheating occurs when someone deceives another person by fraudulently or dishonestly inducing them to deliver property, consent to retaining property, or do something they wouldn’t normally do, resulting in harm.

paragraphs.context: Explanation: A dishonest concealment of facts also constitutes deception under this section.

qas.question: What does the explanation of Section 318 indicate about dishonest concealment of facts?

answers.text: The explanation of Section 318 indicates that a dishonest concealment of facts is also considered deception under this section.

paragraphs.context: Illustrations: Example 1: A pretends to be in the Civil Service and deceives Z, leading Z to give him goods on credit without intending to pay for them. A cheats.

qas.question: Give an example of someone cheating under Section 318.

answers.text: An example of someone cheating under Section 318 is when A pretends to be in the Civil Service and deceives Z into giving him goods on credit without intending to pay for them.

paragraphs.context: Example 2: A puts a counterfeit mark on an article, misleading Z into thinking it is made by a famous manufacturer, thus causing Z to buy it. A cheats.

qas.question: What is another example of cheating under Section 318?

answers.text: Another example of cheating is when A puts a counterfeit mark on an article, misleading Z into thinking it is made by a famous manufacturer, thus causing Z to buy it.

paragraphs.context: Example 3: A shows a false sample of an article to Z, deceiving Z into believing the actual article matches the sample, leading to Z buying it. A cheats.

qas.question: What is another example of cheating under Section 318?

answers.text: Another example of cheating is when A shows a false sample of an article to Z, deceiving Z into believing the actual article matches the sample, leading to Z buying it.

paragraphs.context: Example 4: A uses a dishonored bill to buy an article, intending not to pay for it. A cheats.

qas.question: Give an example of cheating related to dishonored bills under Section 318.

answers.text: An example of cheating related to dishonored bills is when A uses a dishonored bill to buy an article, intending not to pay for it.

paragraphs.context: Example 5: A pledges non-diamonds as diamonds to Z, inducing Z to lend money based on the false representation. A cheats.

qas.question: What is an example of cheating involving false representation of diamonds under Section 318?

answers.text: An example of cheating involving false representation of diamonds is when A pledges non-diamonds as diamonds to Z, inducing Z to lend money based on the false representation.

paragraphs.context: Example 6: A deceives Z into believing they will repay a loan, but A does not intend to repay. A cheats.

qas.question: What is an example of cheating involving a loan under Section 318?

answers.text: An example of cheating involving a loan is when A deceives Z into believing they will repay a loan, but A does not intend to repay.

paragraphs.context: Example 7: A deceives Z into thinking they will deliver a certain amount of indigo plant, inducing Z to lend money, even though A has no intention of delivering. A cheats.

qas.question: What is an example of cheating involving indigo plants under Section 318?

answers.text: An example of cheating involving indigo plants is when A deceives Z into thinking they will deliver a certain amount of indigo plant, inducing Z to lend money, even though A has no intention of delivering.

paragraphs.context: Example 8: A deceives Z into thinking they have fulfilled their part of a contract, and thus induces Z to pay money. A cheats.

qas.question: What is an example of cheating involving a contract under Section 318?

answers.text: An example of cheating involving a contract is when A deceives Z into thinking they have fulfilled their part of the contract, inducing Z to pay money.

paragraphs.context: Cheating and Punishments: General Cheating: Anyone who cheats is subject to imprisonment for up to 3 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for general cheating?

answers.text: The punishment for general cheating is imprisonment for up to 3 years, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Cheating with Knowledge of Wrongful Loss: If a person cheats, knowing that it will likely cause a wrongful loss to someone whose interests they are legally obligated to protect, the punishment increases to imprisonment for up to 5 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for cheating with knowledge of wrongful loss?

answers.text: The punishment for cheating with knowledge of wrongful loss is imprisonment for up to 5 years, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Cheating and Deception Leading to Property Transfer: If a person cheats and deceives the victim into delivering property, or altering or destroying a valuable security, or anything capable of becoming a valuable security, the punishment is imprisonment for up to 7 years, along with a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for cheating that leads to a property transfer?

answers.text: The punishment for cheating leading to a property transfer is imprisonment for up to 7 years, along with a fine.

paragraphs.context: Definition of Cheating by Personation: A person cheats by pretending to be someone else or by substituting one person for another, representing that they or another person is someone other than they truly are. This offense can occur whether the person being impersonated is real or imaginary.

qas.question: What is cheating by personation?

answers.text: Cheating by personation occurs when a person pretends to be someone else or substitutes one person for another, representing themselves or another person as someone other than they truly are.

paragraphs.context: Illustrations: Example 1: A cheats by pretending to be a rich banker with the same name. This is considered cheating by personation. Example 2: A cheats by pretending to be someone who is deceased. This is also cheating by personation.

qas.question: Give examples of cheating by personation.

answers.text: Example 1: A cheats by pretending to be a rich banker with the same name. Example 2: A cheats by pretending to be someone who is deceased.

paragraphs.context: Punishment: Cheating by personation is punishable by imprisonment for up to 5 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for cheating by personation?

answers.text: The punishment for cheating by personation is imprisonment for up to 5 years, a fine, or both.

data.title: Section 320: Fraudulent Removal or Concealment of Property

paragraphs.context: Dishonest or Fraudulent Transfer of Property: Anyone who dishonestly or fraudulently removes, conceals, delivers, or transfers property without adequate consideration, intending to prevent the distribution of that property according to law among creditors, shall be punished with: Imprisonment for 6 months to 2 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for dishonest or fraudulent transfer of property?

answers.text: The punishment for dishonest or fraudulent transfer of property is imprisonment for 6 months to 2 years, a fine, or both.

data.title: Section 321: Preventing Debts from Being Paid

paragraphs.context: Dishonestly Preventing Debt Payment: A person who dishonestly or fraudulently prevents a debt or demand from being made available for the payment of his debts or another person's debts, shall be punished with: Imprisonment for up to 2 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for dishonestly preventing debt payment?

answers.text: The punishment for dishonestly preventing debt payment is imprisonment for up to 2 years, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Fraudulent Signing or Execution of Deeds: Anyone who dishonestly or fraudulently signs, executes, or becomes a party to a deed or instrument that transfers or subjects property to a charge and contains false statements about the consideration for the transfer or charge, or the person it benefits, shall be punished with: Imprisonment for up to 3 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for fraudulent signing or execution of deeds?

answers.text: The punishment for fraudulent signing or execution of deeds is imprisonment for up to 3 years, a fine, or both.

data.title: Section 323: Concealment or Removal of Property

paragraphs.context: Dishonest or Fraudulent Concealment or Removal: Anyone who dishonestly or fraudulently conceals or removes any property of themselves or another person, or assists in such concealment or removal, or dishonestly releases any demand or claim they are entitled to, shall be punished with: Imprisonment for up to 3 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for dishonest or fraudulent concealment or removal of property?

answers.text: The punishment for dishonest or fraudulent concealment or removal of property is imprisonment for up to 3 years, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Definition of Mischief: Mischief occurs when someone, with intent to cause or knowing they are likely to cause wrongful loss or damage to the public or any person, causes the destruction of property, alters property in a way that diminishes its value, or harms its utility.

qas.question: What is the definition of mischief?

answers.text: Mischief occurs when someone, with intent to cause or knowing they are likely to cause wrongful loss or damage to the public or any person, causes the destruction of property, alters property in a way that diminishes its value, or harms its utility.

paragraphs.context: Explanation 1: The offense doesn't require the intent to cause damage to the property owner specifically. It suffices if the action causes wrongful loss or damage to any person, even if the property does not belong to that person.

qas.question: Does the offense of mischief require the intent to cause damage to the property owner?

answers.text: No, the offense of mischief doesn't require the intent to cause damage to the property owner specifically. It suffices if the action causes wrongful loss or damage to any person.

paragraphs.context: Explanation 2: Mischief can also be committed against property belonging to the person committing the act or jointly with others.

qas.question: Can mischief be committed against property belonging to the person committing the act?

answers.text: Yes, mischief can also be committed against property belonging to the person committing the act or jointly with others.

paragraphs.context: Illustrations: Example 1: A burns a valuable security belonging to Z, intending to cause loss to Z. This is mischief. Example 2: A introduces water into an ice-house owned by Z, causing the ice to melt, with the intent to cause damage to Z. This is mischief.

qas.question: Give examples of mischief.

answers.text: Example 1: A burns a valuable security belonging to Z, intending to cause loss to Z. Example 2: A introduces water into an ice-house owned by Z, causing the ice to melt, with the intent to cause damage to Z.

paragraphs.context: Punishments for Mischief: General Misdeed: Whoever commits mischief shall be punished with imprisonment up to 6 months, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for general mischief?

answers.text: The punishment for general mischief is imprisonment up to 6 months, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Mischief Leading to Loss/Damage of Property: If mischief causes loss or damage to property (including government or local authority property), the punishment is imprisonment up to 1 year, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for mischief leading to loss or damage of property?

answers.text: The punishment for mischief leading to loss or damage of property is imprisonment up to 1 year, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Mischief Leading to Loss/Damage Between ₹20,000 and ₹1 Lakh: The punishment increases to imprisonment up to 2 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for mischief leading to loss or damage between ₹20,000 and ₹1 Lakh?

answers.text: The punishment for mischief leading to loss or damage between ₹20,000 and ₹1 Lakh is imprisonment up to 2 years, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Mischief Leading to Loss/Damage Over ₹1 Lakh: If mischief causes loss or damage to the amount of ₹1 lakh or more, the punishment is imprisonment up to 5 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for mischief leading to loss or damage over ₹1 Lakh?

answers.text: The punishment for mischief leading to loss or damage over ₹1 Lakh is imprisonment up to 5 years, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Mischief in Specific Areas: Mischief by Killing, Poisoning, or Maiming Animals: Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming, or rendering any animal useless will be punished with imprisonment for up to 5 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for mischief by killing, poisoning, or maiming animals?

answers.text: The punishment for mischief by killing, poisoning, or maiming animals is imprisonment for up to 5 years, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Mischief by Affecting Water Supply: Whoever causes or is likely to cause a reduction in the supply of water for agriculture, food, drink, or cleanliness, will face imprisonment for up to 5 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for mischief by affecting water supply?

answers.text: The punishment for mischief by affecting water supply is imprisonment for up to 5 years, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Mischief by Affecting Public Infrastructure: Affecting public roads, bridges, rivers, or navigable channels (rendering them impassable or unsafe): Up to 5 years imprisonment, a fine, or both. Acts causing inundation or obstruction to public drainage: Up to 5 years imprisonment, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for mischief affecting public infrastructure?

answers.text: The punishment for mischief affecting public infrastructure, including roads, bridges, rivers, or navigable channels, is up to 5 years imprisonment, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Mischief Affecting Navigation: Anyone who destroys or moves any sign or signal for the navigation of rail, aircraft, or ships, or makes them less useful, will be punished with: Imprisonment for up to 7 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for mischief affecting navigation signals?

answers.text: The punishment for mischief affecting navigation signals is imprisonment for up to 7 years, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Mischief by Affecting Landmarks: Destroying or moving any landmark fixed by a public authority, or making it less useful, carries a punishment of: Imprisonment for up to 1 year, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for mischief affecting landmarks?

answers.text: The punishment for mischief affecting landmarks is imprisonment for up to 1 year, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Mischief by Fire or Explosives: If someone uses fire or explosives to cause damage to property (including agricultural produce), the punishment is: Imprisonment for up to 7 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for mischief involving fire or explosives?

answers.text: The punishment for mischief involving fire or explosives is imprisonment for up to 7 years, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Mischief by Destroying Places of Worship or Dwelling: If fire or explosives are used to destroy buildings like places of worship, human dwellings, or places for storing property, the punishment is: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for up to 10 years, and a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for destroying places of worship or dwelling using fire or explosives?

answers.text: The punishment for destroying places of worship or dwelling using fire or explosives is imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for up to 10 years, and a fine.

paragraphs.context: Mischief Involving Rail, Aircraft, or Vessels: Destruction or Unsafe Rendering of Transport Infrastructure: If someone commits mischief to destroy or make unsafe any rail, aircraft, or vessel (of 20 tons or more), the punishment is: Imprisonment for up to 10 years, a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for mischief involving rail, aircraft, or vessels of 20 tons or more?

answers.text: The punishment for mischief involving rail, aircraft, or vessels of 20 tons or more is imprisonment for up to 10 years, a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Mischief by Fire or Explosives: If the mischief involves fire or explosives, the punishment increases: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for up to 10 years, and a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for mischief involving fire or explosives on transport infrastructure?

answers.text: The punishment for mischief involving fire or explosives on transport infrastructure is imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for up to 10 years, and a fine.

data.title: Section 328: Mischief by Intentionally Running a Vessel Aground

paragraphs.context: Mischief Involving a Vessel: If someone intentionally runs a vessel aground or ashore with the purpose of committing theft or dishonestly misappropriating any property within the vessel, or with the intent that such theft or misappropriation may be committed, the punishment is: Imprisonment for up to 10 years, and a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for intentionally running a vessel aground with the purpose of committing theft or misappropriation?

answers.text: The punishment for intentionally running a vessel aground with the purpose of committing theft or misappropriation is imprisonment for up to 10 years, and a fine.

paragraphs.context: Criminal Trespass Defined: Criminal trespass occurs when someone enters into or remains upon property in the possession of another person with the intent to commit an offense or to intimidate, insult, or annoy the person in possession of the property. A person who unlawfully remains on such property with intent to commit an offense or intimidate the person is also considered to be committing criminal trespass.

qas.question: What constitutes criminal trespass?

answers.text: Criminal trespass occurs when someone enters or remains upon property in the possession of another person with the intent to commit an offense or to intimidate, insult, or annoy the person in possession of the property.

paragraphs.context: House-Trespass: If criminal trespass is committed by entering or remaining in any building, tent, vessel used as a human dwelling, or a building used as a place of worship or for the custody of property, it is considered house-trespass.

qas.question: What is considered house-trespass?

answers.text: House-trespass occurs if criminal trespass is committed by entering or remaining in any building, tent, vessel used as a human dwelling, or a building used as a place of worship or for the custody of property.

paragraphs.context: Punishments for Criminal Trespass: Imprisonment for up to 3 months, a fine of up to 5,000 rupees, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for criminal trespass?

answers.text: The punishment for criminal trespass is imprisonment for up to 3 months, a fine of up to 5,000 rupees, or both.

paragraphs.context: Punishments for House-Trespass: Imprisonment for up to 1 year, a fine of up to 5,000 rupees, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for house-trespass?

answers.text: The punishment for house-trespass is imprisonment for up to 1 year, a fine of up to 5,000 rupees, or both.

paragraphs.context: Lurking House-Trespass: Lurking house-trespass is committed when someone engages in house-trespass while taking precautions to conceal their actions from a person who has the right to exclude or eject the trespasser from the building, tent, or vessel involved.

qas.question: What constitutes lurking house-trespass?

answers.text: Lurking house-trespass occurs when someone engages in house-trespass while taking precautions to conceal their actions from the person who has the right to exclude or eject the trespasser.

paragraphs.context: Housebreaking Defined: A person commits housebreaking if they commit house-trespass by entering or exiting a house or part of it in any of the following ways: Creating a Passage: Entering or exiting through a passage made by the trespasser or an accomplice. Unauthorized Passage: Entering or exiting through a passage not intended by anyone other than the trespasser or accomplice, such as scaling or climbing over walls. Opening a Passage: Entering or exiting through a passage opened by the trespasser or accomplice that was not meant to be opened by the occupier. Opening a Lock: Entering or exiting by opening a lock to facilitate the house-trespass. Using Force or Assault: Entering or exiting by using criminal force or committing an assault, or by threatening to do so. Unfastening a Locked Passage: Entering or exiting by a passage that was fastened against entry but was unfastened by the trespasser or accomplice.

qas.question: What constitutes housebreaking?

answers.text: Housebreaking occurs when someone commits house-trespass by entering or exiting a house or part of it by creating a passage, unauthorized passage, opening a passage, opening a lock, using force or assault, or unfastening a locked passage.

data.title: Explanation and Punishments for Lurking House-Trespass and Housebreaking

paragraphs.context: Explanation on House-Trespass and Housebreaking: Out-Houses and Internal Communication: Any out-house or building connected to a house, with immediate internal communication between them, is considered part of the house within the meaning of house-trespass and housebreaking.

qas.question: What is considered part of the house in terms of house-trespass and housebreaking?

answers.text: Any out-house or building connected to a house, with immediate internal communication between them, is considered part of the house within the meaning of house-trespass and housebreaking.

paragraphs.context: Illustrations of House-Trespass and Housebreaking: Example Scenarios: (a) A makes a hole in the wall of Z’s house and puts his hand through. This constitutes house-breaking. (b) A creeps into a ship at a port-hole between decks. This is house-breaking. (c) A enters Z’s house through a window. This is house-breaking. (d) A enters Z’s house by opening a fastened door. This is house-breaking. (e) A lifts a latch with a wire through a hole to open the door and enters Z’s house. This is house-breaking. (f) A finds the key to Z’s house and enters by using that key. This is house-breaking. (g) A knocks Z down and forces a passage into Z’s house. This is house-breaking. (h) A enters Z’s house after threatening to beat Z, the door-keeper. This is house-breaking.

answers.text: This constitutes house-breaking.

answers.text: This is house-breaking.

answers.text: This is house-breaking.

answers.text: This is house-breaking.

answers.text: This is house-breaking.

answers.text: This is house-breaking.

answers.text: This is house-breaking.
