answers.text: This is house-breaking.

paragraphs.context: Punishments for Lurking House-Trespass and Housebreaking: General Punishment: Anyone committing lurking house-trespass or housebreaking can be punished with: Imprisonment for up to 2 years, and also liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the general punishment for lurking house-trespass or housebreaking?

answers.text: Anyone committing lurking house-trespass or housebreaking can be punished with imprisonment for up to 2 years, and also liable to fine.

paragraphs.context: Punishment after Sunset and Before Sunrise: If the lurking house-trespass or housebreaking occurs after sunset and before sunrise, the punishment is: Imprisonment for up to 3 years, and also liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment if lurking house-trespass or housebreaking occurs after sunset and before sunrise?

answers.text: The punishment is imprisonment for up to 3 years, and also liable to fine.

paragraphs.context: Punishment for Commission with Intent to Commit Another Offense: If the house-trespass or housebreaking is committed with the intent to commit another offense punishable with imprisonment: Imprisonment for up to 3 years, and also liable to fine. If the intended offense is theft, the imprisonment term can be extended to 10 years.

qas.question: What is the punishment if house-trespass or housebreaking is committed with the intent to commit theft?

answers.text: If the intended offense is theft, the imprisonment term can be extended to 10 years.

paragraphs.context: Punishment after Sunset and Before Sunrise (Intent to Commit an Offense): If the offense occurs after sunset and before sunrise, and is committed to facilitate another offense punishable with imprisonment: Imprisonment for up to 5 years, and also liable to fine. If the intended offense is theft, the term of imprisonment may extend to 14 years.

qas.question: What is the punishment if the offense occurs after sunset and before sunrise, and is committed to facilitate theft?

answers.text: The term of imprisonment may extend to 14 years if the intended offense is theft.

paragraphs.context: Punishment for Preparedness to Cause Hurt or Assault: If the house-trespass or housebreaking is committed with preparation to cause hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or to instill fear of hurt, the punishment can be: Imprisonment for up to 10 years, and also liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment if house-trespass or housebreaking is committed with preparation to cause hurt or assault?

answers.text: The punishment can be imprisonment for up to 10 years, and also liable to fine.

paragraphs.context: Punishment after Sunset and Before Sunrise (Preparedness for Hurt or Assault): If the house-trespass or housebreaking occurs after sunset and before sunrise, with preparation for causing hurt, assault, or wrongful restraint, the punishment is: Imprisonment for up to 14 years, and also liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for house-trespass or housebreaking after sunset and before sunrise, with preparation for causing hurt or assault?

answers.text: The punishment is imprisonment for up to 14 years, and also liable to fine.

data.title: Punishments and Provisions for House-Trespass and Related Offenses

paragraphs.context: Punishments for Lurking House-Trespass or Housebreaking with Grievous Harm: Grievous Hurt or Attempt to Cause Death: If a person, while committing lurking house-trespass or housebreaking, causes grievous hurt to someone or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt, the punishment is: Imprisonment for life, or Imprisonment for a term up to 10 years, along with a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for causing grievous hurt or attempting to cause death or grievous hurt during lurking house-trespass or housebreaking?

answers.text: Imprisonment for life, or Imprisonment for a term up to 10 years, along with a fine.

paragraphs.context: Grievous Hurt or Attempt to Cause Death (After Sunset and Before Sunrise): If the offense occurs after sunset and before sunrise, and the person voluntarily causes or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt, everyone involved in the offense will face: Imprisonment for life, or Imprisonment for a term up to 10 years, and a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment if grievous hurt or attempt to cause death occurs after sunset and before sunrise during house-trespass or housebreaking?

answers.text: Imprisonment for life, or Imprisonment for a term up to 10 years, and a fine.

paragraphs.context: House-Trespass to Commit Other Offenses: Section 332 outlines the punishments for house-trespass with the intent to commit another offense: (a) Punishable with death: Imprisonment for life, or Rigorous imprisonment for up to 10 years, and a fine. (b) Punishable with life imprisonment: Imprisonment for a term up to 10 years, and a fine. (c) Punishable with imprisonment: Imprisonment for up to 2 years, and a fine. If the intended offense is theft, the imprisonment term may extend to 7 years.

qas.question: What is the punishment for house-trespass to commit another offense that is punishable with death?

answers.text: Imprisonment for life, or Rigorous imprisonment for up to 10 years, and a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for house-trespass to commit another offense punishable with life imprisonment?

answers.text: Imprisonment for a term up to 10 years, and a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for house-trespass to commit another offense punishable with imprisonment?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to 2 years, and a fine.

paragraphs.context: Punishments for House-Trespass with Preparation for Harm: Preparation for Causing Harm: If house-trespass is committed with preparations for: Causing hurt, Assaulting, Wrongfully restraining, Instilling fear of harm or restraint, The punishment is: Imprisonment for up to 7 years, and a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for house-trespass committed with preparations for causing harm?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to 7 years, and a fine.

paragraphs.context: Punishments for Breaking Open or Unfastening Receptacles: Section 334 outlines the penalties for breaking open or unfastening closed receptacles: Dishonestly or with intent to commit mischief: Breaking or unfastening a closed receptacle (that may contain property or that the person believes to contain property) results in: Imprisonment for up to 2 years, or a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for breaking open or unfastening a closed receptacle dishonestly or with intent to commit mischief?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to 2 years, or a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Being entrusted with a closed receptacle: If a person, without authority, dishonestly or with intent to commit mischief, breaks open or unfastens a receptacle they were entrusted with, the punishment is: Imprisonment for up to 3 years, or a fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for breaking open or unfastening a receptacle one was entrusted with, without authority, with intent to commit mischief?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to 3 years, or a fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Offenses Related to Making False Documents: Section 335 defines the offense of making false documents: A person commits making a false document or electronic record if they: Dishonestly or fraudulently: Make, sign, seal, or execute a document or part of a document. Make or transmit any electronic record or part of it. Affix an electronic signature on any electronic record. Make any mark denoting the execution of a document or the authenticity of the electronic signature. The intent is to cause the belief that the document, electronic record, or signature was made, signed, sealed, executed, transmitted, or affixed by the authorized person or authority.

qas.question: What is the offense related to making false documents or electronic records?

answers.text: Dishonestly or fraudulently making, signing, sealing, or executing a document or part of a document, transmitting an electronic record, affixing an electronic signature, or making a mark denoting execution or authenticity.

paragraphs.context: Section 335 further elaborates on the offense of making false documents or electronic records. A person commits forgery in the following ways: A. Dishonest Creation or Execution of False Documents. A person who dishonestly or fraudulently makes, signs, seals, or executes a document or an electronic record with the intent to deceive another person into believing it to be genuine when it is not. This includes: Making or transmitting any part of a document or electronic record. Affixing an electronic signature to a document without lawful authority. Creating a mark that suggests authenticity or execution of the document.

qas.question: What actions constitute dishonest creation or execution of false documents or electronic records?

answers.text: Making, signing, sealing, or executing a document or electronic record with the intent to deceive, including affixing an electronic signature without lawful authority or creating a mark suggesting authenticity.

paragraphs.context: B. Altering a Document or Electronic Record Without Authority. Dishonestly or fraudulently altering a document or electronic record: This could be through cancellation or any other method of altering the content of the document, after it has been made, signed, or affixed with an electronic signature. The alteration can be made either by the same person who originally executed it or by another person, even if that person is alive or deceased at the time of alteration.

qas.question: What actions are considered altering a document or electronic record without authority?

answers.text: Dishonestly or fraudulently altering a document or electronic record, such as cancellation or changing its content after it has been signed or affixed with an electronic signature.

paragraphs.context: C. Inducing a Person to Alter or Sign a Document Fraudulently. A person who dishonestly or fraudulently induces someone else to: Sign, seal, execute, or alter a document or electronic record, Affix an electronic signature, knowing that the person: Cannot comprehend the document or its alteration due to unsoundness of mind or intoxication, Is deceived, and does not know the contents of the document or the nature of the alteration.

qas.question: What constitutes inducing someone to alter or sign a document fraudulently?

answers.text: Dishonestly or fraudulently inducing someone to sign, seal, execute, or alter a document, or affix an electronic signature, knowing the person cannot comprehend the document due to unsoundness of mind or intoxication, or is deceived.

paragraphs.context: Illustrations of Forgery: Example (a): A has a letter of credit for ₹10,000 written by Z. A adds extra digits to make the amount ₹1,00,000, intending to deceive B into believing Z wrote the original amount. A has committed forgery.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (a)?

answers.text: A adds extra digits to a letter of credit, changing ₹10,000 to ₹1,00,000, intending to deceive B.

paragraphs.context: Example (b): A, without Z’s authority, affixes Z’s seal to a conveyance document purporting to transfer an estate to A. A intends to sell the estate to B and defraud B of the purchase money. A has committed forgery.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (b)?

answers.text: A affixes Z’s seal to a conveyance document without authority, intending to sell the estate to B and defraud B.

paragraphs.context: Example (c): A picks up a cheque signed by B, payable to bearer, but with no sum inserted. A fraudulently fills in the amount of ₹10,000. A has committed forgery.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (c)?

answers.text: A fills in the amount ₹10,000 on a cheque signed by B, without authorization.

paragraphs.context: Example (d): A gives B a cheque with A’s signature but without the sum inserted, authorizing B to fill in the amount not exceeding ₹10,000. However, B fraudulently fills in ₹20,000. B has committed forgery.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (d)?

answers.text: B fraudulently fills in ₹20,000 instead of the authorized ₹10,000 on a cheque.

paragraphs.context: Example (e): A draws a bill of exchange in B's name without B’s authority, intending to deceive a banker into discounting the bill. A is guilty of forgery.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (e)?

answers.text: A draws a bill of exchange in B's name without authority, intending to deceive a banker.

paragraphs.context: Example (f): A alters Z’s will, scratching out B’s name, intending to deceive others into believing that the estate was left solely to A and C. A has committed forgery.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (f)?

answers.text: A alters Z’s will by scratching out B’s name, intending to deceive others.

paragraphs.context: Example (g): B fraudulently erases the words “Pay to Z or his order” from a Government promissory note, changing it to a blank endorsement. B has committed forgery.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (g)?

answers.text: B erases the words 'Pay to Z or his order' from a Government promissory note, changing it to a blank endorsement.

paragraphs.context: Example (h): A sells an estate to Z and later executes a second conveyance to B with a backdated document, intending to deceive Z into believing B received the estate before Z. A has committed forgery.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (h)?

answers.text: A executes a backdated conveyance to B, intending to deceive Z.

paragraphs.context: Example (i): A writes a will for Z, intentionally changing the legatee’s name. A deceives Z into signing the altered will. A has committed forgery.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (i)?

answers.text: A writes a will for Z, changing the legatee’s name and deceiving Z into signing the altered will.

paragraphs.context: Example (j): A writes a letter and signs it with B’s name without authorization, to fraudulently claim that A is in distressed circumstances and seek alms. A has committed forgery.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (j)?

answers.text: A signs a letter with B’s name without authorization, claiming distressed circumstances to seek alms.

paragraphs.context: Section 336 expands on the definition and punishment for forgery: False Documents or Electronic Records with Intent to Cause Harm. Forgery involves creating or altering a false document or electronic record with the intent to cause damage or injury: To the public or any individual. To support a claim or title. To cause someone to part with property. To enter into a contract (express or implied). To commit fraud or facilitate fraud.

qas.question: What constitutes forgery in the context of false documents or electronic records with intent to cause harm?

answers.text: Creating or altering a false document or electronic record with the intent to cause damage or injury to the public, an individual, or for fraud purposes.

paragraphs.context: Punishments for Forgery: For general forgery (making a false document or electronic record), the punishment is: Imprisonment for a term up to two years, or a fine, or both. Forgery with Intent to Cheat: If the forged document or electronic record is intended to be used for cheating, the punishment is: Imprisonment for a term that may extend to seven years, along with a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for general forgery and forgery with intent to cheat?

answers.text: For general forgery: imprisonment up to two years, a fine, or both. For forgery with intent to cheat: imprisonment up to seven years and a fine.

paragraphs.context: Explanation and Additional Illustrations: Forgery Through False Names or Antedating Documents. Illustration (k): A writes a letter in B's name certifying A’s character, with the intention of deceiving Z to obtain employment. A commits forgery because A intended to deceive Z into forming an employment contract based on the forged certificate.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Illustration (k)?

answers.text: A writes a letter in B's name to deceive Z into obtaining employment, committing forgery.

paragraphs.context: Explanation 1 - Signing Own Name: A person’s signature of their own name may constitute forgery if it is intended to deceive. Example (a): A signs their own name on a bill of exchange, intending that others believe the bill was drawn by someone else with the same name. A commits forgery.

qas.question: Can signing one's own name be considered forgery?

answers.text: Yes, if it is intended to deceive others into believing it was signed by someone else with the same name.

paragraphs.context: Explanation 2 - Forgery in the Name of Fictional or Deceased Persons: Making a document in the name of a fictitious person or a deceased person, intending to mislead others into believing it was created by the person during their lifetime, can be forgery. Example: A draws a bill of exchange upon a fictitious person and fraudulently accepts it in that name to negotiate the bill. A commits forgery.

qas.question: What is an example of forgery in the name of fictional or deceased persons?

answers.text: A draws a bill of exchange in the name of a fictitious person and fraudulently accepts it to negotiate the bill.

paragraphs.context: Explanation 3 - Electronic Signature Definition: The term 'affixing electronic signature' is defined in the Information Technology Act, 2000, which helps clarify the legal aspect of electronic records and documents.

qas.question: What is the definition of affixing an electronic signature?

answers.text: The term 'affixing electronic signature' is defined in the Information Technology Act, 2000, which clarifies the legal aspect of electronic records.

paragraphs.context: Examples of Forgery in Other Contexts: Example (b): A writes the word 'accepted' on a piece of paper and signs it with Z’s name, intending for B to draw a bill of exchange on it and negotiate it as if it were accepted by Z. A commits forgery, and B is also guilty if they knowingly participate in the deception.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (b)?

answers.text: A writes 'accepted' on a piece of paper, signs it with Z's name, and intends for B to use it to deceive others into negotiating the bill.

paragraphs.context: Example (c): A picks up a bill of exchange payable to the order of someone else with the same name, and A endorses it intending to deceive others into believing it was endorsed by the intended person. A commits forgery.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (c)?

answers.text: A endorses a bill of exchange payable to someone else with the same name, intending to deceive others.

paragraphs.context: Example (d): B colludes with Z to execute a lease of an estate after it was seized by A under legal execution, dating the lease six months earlier than the seizure to make it appear valid before the property was taken. B commits forgery by antedating the lease.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (d)?

answers.text: B colludes with Z to antedate a lease of an estate seized by A, making it appear valid before the seizure.

paragraphs.context: Example (e): A, anticipating insolvency, lodges goods with B and fraudulently creates a promissory note, backdating it to make it seem like a legitimate transaction before insolvency, in an attempt to defraud creditors. A commits forgery.

qas.question: What is the forgery committed in Example (e)?

answers.text: A creates a backdated promissory note to deceive creditors into believing the transaction occurred before insolvency.

paragraphs.context: Section 336(4) - Harm to Reputation. Forgery with Intent to Harm Reputation: If the forged document or electronic record is created with the intent to harm the reputation of any party, or if the person knows it is likely to be used for such purpose, the punishment is: Imprisonment for a term up to three years, and a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for forgery with intent to harm reputation?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to three years, and a fine.

paragraphs.context: Section 337 - Forging Court Documents or Identity Documents. Forgery of Court or Government Documents: Forgery of a document or electronic record that purports to be a court record, identity document (such as voter ID or Aadhaar card), birth, marriage, or burial register, document by a public servant in their official capacity, or any related document (such as authority to institute a suit or to defend, etc.) is punishable by: Imprisonment for a term up to seven years, and a fine. Explanation: The term “register” includes any list, data, or record maintained in electronic form, as per the Information Technology Act, 2000.

qas.question: What is the punishment for forgery of court or government documents?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to seven years, and a fine.

paragraphs.context: Section 338 - Forgery of Valuable Documents. Forgery of Valuable Documents: Forgery of valuable documents such as: A valuable security (like bonds, shares). A will. Authority to adopt a son. Documents authorizing the transfer or receipt of valuable securities, money, or property. Acquittances or receipts acknowledging the payment of money or delivery of property. Punishment for this type of forgery is severe: Imprisonment for life or up to ten years, along with a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for forgery of valuable documents?

answers.text: Imprisonment for life or up to ten years, along with a fine.

paragraphs.context: Section 339 - Possession of Forged Documents. Possession of Forged Documents: If a person possesses any forged document or electronic record, knowing that it is forged, and intends to use it fraudulently or dishonestly as genuine, the punishment depends on the type of document: Forged Court or Government Documents (as described in Section 337): Imprisonment for up to seven years, along with a fine. Forged Valuable Documents (as described in Section 338): Imprisonment for life, or up to seven years, along with a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for possession of forged documents?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to seven years, and a fine for forged Court or Government documents. Imprisonment for life or up to seven years, along with a fine for forged valuable documents.

paragraphs.context: Section 340 - Use of Forged Documents. Fraudulent Use of Forged Documents: If someone fraudulently or dishonestly uses a document or electronic record that they know or have reason to believe is forged, they are punishable in the same manner as if they themselves had forged the document.

qas.question: What is the punishment for fraudulent use of forged documents?

answers.text: They are punishable in the same manner as if they themselves had forged the document.

paragraphs.context: Section 341 - Counterfeiting Seals, Plates, or Instruments. Counterfeiting Seals or Instruments for Forgery: Making or counterfeiting any seal, plate, or instrument with the intent to use it for committing forgery under Section 338 (e.g., forging valuable documents) is punishable by: Imprisonment for life or up to seven years, and a fine. Counterfeiting for Other Forgeries: Making or counterfeiting a seal or instrument to commit forgery (not under Section 338) is punishable by: Imprisonment for up to seven years, and a fine. Possession of Counterfeit Seals or Instruments: Possession of any counterfeit seal, plate, or instrument (knowing it to be counterfeit) is punishable by: Imprisonment for up to three years, and a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for counterfeiting seals or instruments?

answers.text: Imprisonment for life or up to seven years, and a fine for counterfeiting seals or instruments for forgery. Imprisonment for up to seven years, and a fine for counterfeiting for other forgeries. Imprisonment for up to three years, and a fine for possession of counterfeit seals or instruments.

paragraphs.context: Section 341 - Use of Counterfeit Seals or Instruments. Fraudulent Use of Counterfeit Seals, Plates, or Instruments: Using any counterfeit seal, plate, or instrument (knowing it to be counterfeit) as though it were genuine is punishable in the same manner as if the person had made or counterfeited the seal, plate, or instrument. Punishment: The person is subject to the same penalties as for counterfeiting.

qas.question: What is the punishment for using counterfeit seals or instruments?

answers.text: The person is subject to the same penalties as for counterfeiting.

paragraphs.context: Section 342 - Counterfeiting Devices for Authenticity. Counterfeiting Devices or Marks for Authenticity: Counterfeiting a device or mark on any material (for documents described in Section 338) that is used to authenticate documents, with the intent to give the appearance of authenticity to a forged document is punishable by: Imprisonment for life or up to seven years, and a fine. Possession of Counterfeited Device or Mark: Possessing material with such counterfeit devices or marks, intending them to be used for forgery, is also punishable in the same manner as above.

qas.question: What is the punishment for counterfeiting devices or marks for authenticity?

answers.text: Imprisonment for life or up to seven years, and a fine.

paragraphs.context: Section 343 - Defacing or Destroying Documents. Defacing, Destroying, or Cancelling Important Documents: Fraudulently or dishonestly canceling, destroying, defacing, or secreting documents such as a will, authority to adopt a son, or valuable securities (or attempting to do so) with intent to cause harm or injury, is punishable by: Imprisonment for life or up to seven years, and a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for defacing or destroying important documents?

answers.text: Imprisonment for life or up to seven years, and a fine.

paragraphs.context: Section 344 - Forging or Altering Documents in Employment. Clerks, Officers, or Servants Falsifying Documents: Clerks, officers, or servants, or those acting in such capacity, who wilfully and with intent to defraud: Destroy, alter, mutilate, or falsify documents, records, or accounts belonging to their employer, or Make false entries or alter/remove material particulars from such documents/accounts. The punishment is: Imprisonment for up to seven years, or fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for falsifying documents in employment?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to seven years, or fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Section 342(2) - Counterfeiting Devices for Other Documents. Counterfeiting Devices for Documents Other than Those in Section 338: Counterfeiting a device or mark on any material used to authenticate documents or electronic records (other than those described in Section 338) with the intent to forge documents is punishable by: Imprisonment for up to seven years, and a fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for counterfeiting devices for documents other than those in Section 338?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to seven years, and a fine.

paragraphs.context: Section 345 - Property Marks. Definition of Property Mark: A property mark denotes that movable property belongs to a particular person. Using a false property mark refers to marking movable property, goods, or containers in a manner likely to make others believe that the property belongs to someone other than the actual owner. Punishment for Using a False Property Mark: Imprisonment for up to one year, or fine, or both, unless the accused proves they did not intend to defraud.

qas.question: What is the punishment for using a false property mark?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to one year, or fine, or both, unless the accused proves they did not intend to defraud.

paragraphs.context: Section 346 - Removal, Destruction, or Defacing Property Marks. Removal or Alteration of Property Marks: Removing, destroying, defacing, or adding to any property mark with the intention or knowledge that it may cause injury to any person is punishable by: Imprisonment for up to one year, or fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for removing or altering a property mark?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to one year, or fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Section 347 - Counterfeiting Property Marks. Counterfeiting a Property Mark: Counterfeiting any property mark used by any other person is punishable by: Imprisonment for up to two years, or fine, or both. Counterfeiting a Property Mark Used by a Public Servant: Counterfeiting any property mark used by a public servant or using a counterfeit mark denoting authenticity or quality (e.g., goods manufactured by a specific person, or goods entitled to exemptions) is punishable by: Imprisonment for up to three years, and fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for counterfeiting a property mark?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to two years, or fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Section 348 - Possessing Instruments for Counterfeiting Property Marks. Possessing Instruments or Marks for Counterfeiting: Making or possessing any die, plate, or instrument to counterfeit a property mark, or possessing a property mark to indicate ownership of goods that don’t belong to the owner, is punishable by: Imprisonment for up to three years, or fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for possessing instruments for counterfeiting property marks?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to three years, or fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Section 349 - Selling Goods with Counterfeit Property Marks. Selling or Possessing Goods with Counterfeit Property Marks: Selling or possessing goods with counterfeit property marks, or marks affixed to containers, unless the accused can prove they acted innocently, will be punished by: Imprisonment for up to one year, or fine, or both. Defenses: The defendant can defend by proving that they had no reason to suspect the genuineness of the mark, or that they provided all the relevant information when requested, or that they acted innocently.

qas.question: What is the punishment for selling goods with counterfeit property marks?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to one year, or fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Section 350 - False Marks on Receptacles or Goods. False Marks on Receptacles or Goods: Making false marks on containers or goods in a manner likely to deceive others (e.g., to mislead public servants or others about the contents or quality of goods) is punishable by: Imprisonment for up to three years, or fine, or both. Use of False Marks: Using false marks as described above also carries the same punishment unless the accused can prove they acted without intent to defraud.

qas.question: What is the punishment for making false marks on receptacles or goods?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to three years, or fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Chapter XIX - Criminal Intimidation, Insult, Annoyance, Defamation, etc. Section 351 - Criminal Intimidation. Criminal Intimidation: Threatening another person (or their interests) with injury to their person, reputation, or property (or to someone they care about), with the intent to: Cause alarm or fear in that person, or Compel them to do or omit an act that they are legally bound to do or entitled to do, This constitutes criminal intimidation.

qas.question: What constitutes criminal intimidation?

answers.text: Threatening another person (or their interests) with injury to their person, reputation, or property, with the intent to cause alarm or fear or compel them to do or omit an act they are legally bound to do.

data.title: Criminal Intimidation and Related Offenses - Continued

paragraphs.context: Section 351 - Explanation and Punishments for Criminal Intimidation. Threats Involving a Deceased Person's Reputation: Threatening to harm the reputation of a deceased person in whom the person threatened has an interest falls under this section. Criminal Intimidation by Threatening Harm: General Threat: A person who threatens another (with intent to cause alarm or coerce action) is punishable with: Imprisonment for up to two years, or fine, or both. Criminal Intimidation with Serious Threats: Serious threats involving: Death, grievous hurt, destruction by fire, or offenses punishable with life imprisonment or death, or Imputing unchastity to a woman, shall be punishable by: Imprisonment for up to seven years, or fine, or both. Criminal Intimidation by Anonymous Threat: Anonymous threats or threats made while concealing the identity of the threatener shall carry an additional punishment of: Imprisonment for up to two years, along with any other punishment applicable to the offense under section (1).

qas.question: What is the punishment for general criminal intimidation?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to two years, or fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Section 352 - Provocation Leading to Public Disorder. Intentionally Insulting and Provoking: Insulting a person to provoke them into breaking public peace or committing an offense: Imprisonment for up to two years, or fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for insulting someone to provoke public disorder?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to two years, or fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Section 353 - False Statements, Rumors, and Reports. False Statements Leading to Mutiny or Misconduct: Making or circulating false statements or rumors with the intent to: Induce mutiny or cause soldiers, sailors, or airmen to fail in their duties, or Create fear or alarm that could lead to a crime, or Incite violence between communities, is punishable by: Imprisonment for up to three years, or fine, or both. Inciting Hatred or Ill-Will Through False Information: Making or circulating false statements that promote enmity or hatred between different communities or groups (based on religion, race, language, etc.) is punishable by: Imprisonment for up to three years, or fine, or both. Offense Committed in a Place of Worship: If the offense under sub-section (2) is committed in a place of worship or during religious ceremonies, the punishment is: Imprisonment for up to five years, and fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for making false statements that lead to mutiny or misconduct?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to three years, or fine, or both.

data.title: Criminal Intimidation, Insult, and Defamation - Continued

paragraphs.context: Section 353 - Exception for False Statements. Exception for Good Faith: A person making, publishing, or circulating a false statement, information, rumor, or report will not be considered an offense if they had reasonable grounds to believe the statement was true and acted in good faith without any intent to cause harm or incite fear.

qas.question: What is the exception for making false statements under Section 353?

answers.text: A person will not be considered an offense if they had reasonable grounds to believe the statement was true and acted in good faith without any intent to cause harm or incite fear.

paragraphs.context: Section 354 - Inducing Belief in Divine Displeasure. Inducing Belief in Divine Displeasure: Whoever causes or attempts to cause someone to do something, or not to do something, by inducing them to believe that their actions (or omissions) will incur Divine displeasure will be punished with: Imprisonment for up to one year, or fine, or both. Illustrations: Example 1: A person sits outside someone's door (dharnas) with the intention of causing the other person to believe that their inaction would lead to Divine displeasure. Example 2: A person threatens harm to their own child, suggesting that the harm will lead to Divine displeasure for the person who does not act according to their wishes.

qas.question: What is the punishment for inducing belief in Divine displeasure?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to one year, or fine, or both.

paragraphs.context: Section 355 - Intoxication and Annoyance in Public Places. Public Intoxication and Annoyance: Intoxicated individuals causing annoyance in public places or trespassing areas they are not permitted to enter shall be punished with: Simple imprisonment for up to 24 hours, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both, or community service.

qas.question: What is the punishment for public intoxication and annoyance?

answers.text: Simple imprisonment for up to 24 hours, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both, or community service.

paragraphs.context: Section 356 - Defamation. Definition of Defamation: Defamation occurs when someone makes or publishes words (spoken, written, or visual) that impute something negative to a person's reputation, intending to harm it or knowing that it will harm the person's reputation. Examples and Explanations: Imputation about a Deceased Person: If an imputation made about a deceased person would harm their reputation if they were alive, and the intention is to harm their family's feelings, it is considered defamation. Imputation about a Group or Association: A defamatory imputation can be made about a company, association, or collective group. Imputation in the Form of an Irony: An imputation expressed ironically or as an alternative may still be defamatory. What Constitutes Harmful Reputation: An imputation is considered defamatory if it lowers the moral or intellectual character of the person, damages their caste or occupation, or affects their credit or reputation. Example: Example 1: Saying 'Z is an honest man; he never stole B's watch' while implying that Z actually stole the watch is defamation. Example 2: Pointing to Z when asked who stole the watch, with the intent to make others believe that Z did it, is defamation.

qas.question: What is the definition of defamation?

answers.text: Defamation occurs when someone makes or publishes words (spoken, written, or visual) that impute something negative to a person's reputation, intending to harm it or knowing that it will harm the person's reputation.

paragraphs.context: Illustration for Defamation: Example (c): A person who draws a picture of Z running away with B’s watch, intending it to be believed that Z stole B’s watch, is committing defamation unless it falls within one of the exceptions listed below.

qas.question: What is the example for defamation in this section?

answers.text: A person who draws a picture of Z running away with B’s watch, intending it to be believed that Z stole B’s watch, is committing defamation unless it falls within one of the exceptions.

paragraphs.context: Exceptions to Defamation: Exception 1 - Truth for Public Good: It is not defamation if the imputation made is true concerning a person and it is for the public good that the imputation is made or published. Note: Whether it is for the public good is a question of fact (i.e., it depends on the circumstances and evidence).

qas.question: What is the first exception to defamation?

answers.text: It is not defamation if the imputation made is true concerning a person and it is for the public good that the imputation is made or published.

paragraphs.context: Exception 2 - Opinions on Public Servants' Conduct: It is not defamation to express in good faith an opinion about a public servant’s conduct in the discharge of their public functions, or about their character as it appears in that conduct (and no further).

qas.question: What does Exception 2 state about public servants?

answers.text: It is not defamation to express in good faith an opinion about a public servant’s conduct in the discharge of their public functions, or about their character as it appears in that conduct (and no further).

paragraphs.context: Exception 3 - Opinions on Public Matters: It is not defamation to express in good faith an opinion about the conduct of any person related to a public question, and their character as it appears in that conduct (and no further). Illustration: If A expresses an opinion in good faith regarding Z's conduct in petitioning the government or participating in a public meeting, it is not defamation.

qas.question: What is Exception 3 about expressing opinions?

answers.text: It is not defamation to express in good faith an opinion about the conduct of any person related to a public question, and their character as it appears in that conduct (and no further).

paragraphs.context: Exception 4 - True Reports of Court Proceedings: It is not defamation to publish a substantially true report of the proceedings of a Court, or the result of any such proceedings. Explanation: A Magistrate or any officer holding an inquiry in open Court (preliminary to a trial) is considered a Court under this section.

qas.question: What is Exception 4 about reports of court proceedings?

answers.text: It is not defamation to publish a substantially true report of the proceedings of a Court, or the result of any such proceedings.

paragraphs.context: Exception 5 - Opinions on Merits of Court Decisions: It is not defamation to express in good faith an opinion regarding the merits of any civil or criminal case decided by a Court, or to express an opinion about the conduct of any person involved in the case (party, witness, agent), regarding their character as it appears in their conduct in the case (and no further). Illustrations: (a): A says, 'I think Z's evidence on that trial is so contradictory that he must be stupid or dishonest'—A is within this exception if said in good faith. (b): A says, 'I do not believe what Z asserted at that trial because I know him to be a man without veracity'—A is not within this exception as it reflects an opinion on Z’s general character, not just his conduct in the trial.

qas.question: What is Exception 5 about expressing opinions on court decisions?

answers.text: It is not defamation to express in good faith an opinion regarding the merits of any civil or criminal case decided by a Court, or to express an opinion about the conduct of any person involved in the case regarding their character as it appears in their conduct in the case.

paragraphs.context: Exception 6 - Opinions on Public Performances: It is not defamation to express in good faith an opinion about the merits of a public performance (such as a book, speech, or stage performance), or about the character of the performer as it appears in the performance (and no further). Explanation: A performance is considered as submitted to the public judgment either explicitly (e.g., publishing a book) or implicitly (e.g., acting in a public play). Illustrations: (a): A book published by an author is considered submitted to the public's judgment. (b): A public speech is submitted to public judgment. (c): A stage performance by an actor or singer is submitted to public judgment.

qas.question: What is Exception 6 about public performances?

answers.text: It is not defamation to express in good faith an opinion about the merits of a public performance (such as a book, speech, or stage performance), or about the character of the performer as it appears in the performance.

paragraphs.context: Illustrations for Exceptions: Example (d): A says about Z's book, 'Z's book is foolish; Z must be a weak man. Z’s book is indecent; Z must be a man of impure mind.' A is within the exception if the opinion expressed is based on Z’s book, in good faith, and respects Z’s character only as it appears in the book. Example (e): A says, 'I am not surprised that Z’s book is foolish and indecent, for he is a weak man and a libertine.' A is not within the exception because the opinion about Z’s character is not based on the book, but rather is a general judgment about Z as a person.

qas.question: What does Example (d) describe about opinions on Z’s book?

answers.text: A is within the exception if the opinion expressed is based on Z’s book, in good faith, and respects Z’s character only as it appears in the book.

paragraphs.context: Additional Exceptions to Defamation: Exception 7 - Authority and Censure: It is not defamation if a person, who has authority over another (either by law or lawful contract), passes good faith censure on that person's conduct in matters to which such lawful authority relates. Illustration: A Judge censuring a witness or officer in court. A parent censuring a child in the presence of other children. A master censuring a servant for remissness in service.

qas.question: What is Exception 7 about authority and censure?

answers.text: It is not defamation if a person, who has authority over another (either by law or lawful contract), passes good faith censure on that person's conduct in matters to which such lawful authority relates.

paragraphs.context: Exception 8 - Accusations to Those with Lawful Authority: It is not defamation to make an accusation in good faith to someone with lawful authority over the accused, related to the subject of the accusation. Illustration: A accuses Z before a Magistrate in good faith. A complains about Z’s conduct as a servant to Z’s employer. A complains about Z’s conduct as a child to Z’s parent.

qas.question: What does Exception 8 state about accusations?

answers.text: It is not defamation to make an accusation in good faith to someone with lawful authority over the accused, related to the subject of the accusation.

paragraphs.context: Exception 9 - Imputation for Protection of Interests: It is not defamation to make an imputation in good faith for the protection of personal or others' interests, or for the public good. Illustrations: (a) A, a shopkeeper, tells B, the manager, not to sell to Z unless Z pays in cash, stating, 'I have no opinion of his honesty.' (b) A, a Magistrate, reports on Z's character to a superior officer in good faith for the public good.

qas.question: What does Exception 9 state about imputations for protection of interests?

answers.text: It is not defamation to make an imputation in good faith for the protection of personal or others' interests, or for the public good.

paragraphs.context: Exception 10 - Conveying a Caution for Good: It is not defamation to caution someone in good faith about another person, provided the caution is for the good of the person receiving it, their interests, or for the public good.

qas.question: What is Exception 10 about conveying a caution for good?

answers.text: It is not defamation to caution someone in good faith about another person, provided the caution is for the good of the person receiving it, their interests, or for the public good.

paragraphs.context: Punishments for Defamation: Punishment for Defamation: Imprisonment for up to two years, or fine, or both, or community service. Punishment for Printing Defamatory Material: If someone prints or engraves defamatory content, knowing or having good reason to believe it is defamatory, they can face: Imprisonment for up to two years, or fine, or both. Punishment for Selling Defamatory Material: Anyone selling or offering for sale material known to be defamatory shall be punished with: Imprisonment for up to two years, or fine, or both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for defamation?

answers.text: Imprisonment for up to two years, or fine, or both, or community service.

qas.question: What happens if someone prints defamatory material?

answers.text: If someone prints or engraves defamatory content, knowing or having good reason to believe it is defamatory, they can face imprisonment for up to two years, or fine, or both.

data.title: Section 357: Neglect of Duty by a Person Bound by a Lawful Contract

paragraphs.context: Punishment for Omission: Whoever is legally bound by a contract to attend to or supply the needs of a person who is helpless, due to youth, unsoundness of mind, or a disease or bodily weakness, and voluntarily omits to perform that duty, shall face: Imprisonment (either description) for up to three months, or Fine up to ₹5,000, or Both imprisonment and fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for neglecting a duty under a lawful contract for a helpless person?

answers.text: Imprisonment (either description) for up to three months, or Fine up to ₹5,000, or Both imprisonment and fine.

paragraphs.context:
Section 358: Repeal and Savings of the Indian Penal Code
Repeal of the Indian Penal Code: The Indian Penal Code (IPC) is officially repealed under this section.
Exceptions to Repeal: Despite the repeal, the following provisions are unaffected: a. Previous Operations: The previous operation of the repealed Code or anything that was lawfully done or suffered under it remains valid. b. Rights and Liabilities: Any rights, privileges, obligations, or liabilities acquired under the repealed Code are still valid. c. Penalties and Punishments: Any penalties or punishments incurred for offenses committed under the repealed Code continue to apply. d. Investigations: Investigations or remedies related to any penalties or punishments incurred remain valid. e. Proceedings: Proceedings, investigations, or remedies related to penalties or punishments incurred may be continued, and penalties can still be imposed as if the Code had not been repealed.

qas.question: What does Section 358 of the Indian Penal Code deal with?

answers.text: The repeal of the Indian Penal Code and the exceptions to the repeal.

paragraphs.context: Actions Under the Repealed Code: Anything done or any action taken under the repealed Indian Penal Code shall be treated as if it was done under the corresponding provisions of the new Code (Sanhita). General Clauses Act, 1897: The repeal of the Indian Penal Code does not affect the general application of Section 6 of the General Clauses Act, 1897, which governs the effects of repeal.

qas.question: What happens to actions done under the repealed Indian Penal Code?

answers.text: Anything done or any action taken under the repealed Indian Penal Code shall be treated as if it was done under the corresponding provisions of the new Code (Sanhita).
